title: Energy Meter Integration via Modbus TCP
app: Splunk Edge Hub (Modbus TCP client), Splunk OT Intelligence
spl:
index=edge-hub-logs sourcetype=edge_hub modbus_register meter_type=energy modbus_register_name=total_energy_kwh
| stats latest(register_value) as total_kwh, latest(_time) as latest_time by meter_id
| join max=1 meter_id [| rest /services/saved/data-model/indexes/energy_meter_cost_model | fields meter_id, cost_per_kwh]
| eval daily_cost=(total_kwh * cost_per_kwh)
| stats sum(daily_cost) as total_daily_cost by meter_id
| where total_daily_cost > threshold
description: Monitors power consumption and demand charges in real-time to identify energy waste and optimize utility costs.
value: Monitors power consumption and demand charges in real-time to identify energy waste and optimize utility costs.
implementation: Deploy Edge Hub with Modbus TCP connectivity to energy meter (Schneider, Siemens, ABB models typical support). Configure register map: 0x0000=voltage, 0x0002=current, 0x0004=power_factor, 0x000C=total_energy_kwh. Set polling interval to 1-5 minutes for demand tracking. Store register values in edge-hub-logs as events or convert to metrics (kW, kVAR) in edge-hub-data for time-series analysis. Local SQLite backlog ensures no consumption data is lost. Implement demand charge alerts (cost spike detection) via SPL.
visualization: Energy consumption trend, cost breakdown by zone, demand charge projection.
knownFalsePositives: Sensors offline during scheduled site visits, calibration, or replacement campaigns.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: Splunk Edge Hub (Modbus TCP client), Splunk OT Intelligence.
• Ensure the following data sources are available: `index=edge-hub-logs` sourcetype=edge_hub modbus_register meter_type=energy, `index=edge-hub-data` metric_name=power.
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
Deploy Edge Hub with Modbus TCP connectivity to energy meter (Schneider, Siemens, ABB models typical support). Configure register map: 0x0000=voltage, 0x0002=current, 0x0004=power_factor, 0x000C=total_energy_kwh. Set polling interval to 1-5 minutes for demand tracking. Store register values in edge-hub-logs as events or convert to metrics (kW, kVAR) in edge-hub-data for time-series analysis. Local SQLite backlog ensures no consumption data is lost. Implement demand charge alerts (cost spike dete…

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=edge-hub-logs sourcetype=edge_hub modbus_register meter_type=energy modbus_register_name=total_energy_kwh
| stats latest(register_value) as total_kwh, latest(_time) as latest_time by meter_id
| join max=1 meter_id [| rest /services/saved/data-model/indexes/energy_meter_cost_model | fields meter_id, cost_per_kwh]
| eval daily_cost=(total_kwh * cost_per_kwh)
| stats sum(daily_cost) as total_daily_cost by meter_id
| where total_daily_cost > threshold
```

Understanding this SPL

**Energy Meter Integration via Modbus TCP** — Monitors power consumption and demand charges in real-time to identify energy waste and optimize utility costs.

Documented **Data sources**: `index=edge-hub-logs` sourcetype=edge_hub modbus_register meter_type=energy, `index=edge-hub-data` metric_name=power. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Splunk Edge Hub (Modbus TCP client), Splunk OT Intelligence. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: edge-hub-logs; **sourcetype**: edge_hub. That sourcetype matches what this use case lists under Data sources.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=edge-hub-logs, sourcetype=edge_hub. Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by meter_id** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions.
• Joins to a subsearch with `join` — set `max=` to match cardinality and avoid silent truncation.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **daily_cost** — often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
• `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by meter_id** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions.
• Filters the current rows with `where total_daily_cost > threshold` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.


Step 3 — Validate
Confirm metric or event volume in the time range, then compare trends and device status to the Splunk Edge Hub admin UI or the mobile app for the same devices. Verify metric names, hosts, and time skew.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Energy consumption trend, cost breakdown by zone, demand charge projection.
grandmaExplanation: We watch what edge sensors report from the physical world. We help you know if something is off with this area: energy meter integration via modbus tcp before harm, damage, or wasted effort piles up.
dataModelAcceleration: Not applicable (no CIM or Operational_Telemetry data model in this SPL).
